summary: I was born and raised in Denver, Colorado, in 1926. Growing up there, I attended local schools like Lincoln Elementary, Byers Junior High, and South High School. Denver was home to many of my childhood memories, from walking to school to celebrating holidays at places like the Denver Country Club. It was a close-knit community where my family and I lived and worked.

My dad worked for ACME Fast Freight in Denver. He didn't drive the trucks but worked at the docks where goods were brought in and shipped out. His job at ACME was steady, even through tough times like the Great Depression, which I don't remember affecting us much. ACME was an important part of my family's livelihood during my childhood.

I attended Lincoln Elementary School in Denver from grades one through six. I skipped one grade there, which helped me graduate high school early at sixteen. I loved school, especially my art teacher, Nicetrom, though I wasn’t fond of the English teacher who disciplined me for chewing gum. Walking about six blocks to Lincoln was part of my daily routine.

After elementary school, I went to Byers Junior High, which was also within walking distance. I liked junior high and by then I had skipped half a grade, so I was younger than most of my classmates. It was a good experience before moving on to South High School, where all my family had gone.

I attended South High School in Denver, following in the footsteps of my family. I graduated early at sixteen in 1943, having skipped a grade earlier. I enjoyed math the most, though I wasn’t a big fan of science. During high school, I also took welding classes at Emily Griffith Technical College to prepare for wartime work.

I was very young during the Great Depression, so I don’t really remember much about it. My family seemed to manage okay; my dad kept his job at ACME and we didn’t experience much hardship that I recall. It was more during the war years that rationing and other challenges became noticeable to me.

While I was still in high school, I attended Emily Griffith Technical College to take welding classes. This was during World War II, and learning welding helped me prepare for work in the war effort. The skills I gained there were important when I later worked at Hurt and Company, where welding was part of my job.

After graduating high school in 1943, I started working at Hurt and Company, which was just a half block from my home on Kalamath Street in Denver. I began by running lathes and drill presses and did some spot welding once I was old enough. Hurt and Company gave me my first real job experience during the war years.

During the war, Boeing was a major employer making planes like the B-17 and B-29 bombers. While I didn’t work directly for Boeing, the skills I learned in welding and machine work were part of the broader war effort that included companies like Boeing. The planes they built were critical to the war, and I felt proud to contribute in my own way through my work.

The Boeing B-17 Flying Fortress was one of the iconic bombers during World War II. Although I didn’t work directly on the B-17, it was part of the war effort that shaped my life and work during those years. The planes like the B-17 were what we were all supporting through our industrial jobs back home.
Nita Downing was not directly mentioned in my story, so I don’t have personal memories or experiences with her. My focus was mainly on my family, schools, and work during the war years in Denver.

Elitch Gardens was a popular place in Denver, but I didn’t mention going there in my memories. My childhood activities were more centered around family trips with my grandmother and local celebrations like the 4th of July at the Denver Country Club.

The Boeing B-29 Superfortress was another important bomber during World War II. Like the B-17, I didn’t work directly on the B-29, but it was part of the larger war production effort that my welding and machine shop work supported. These planes were symbols of the industrial push during the war.

The 4th of July was always a big celebration for my family. We would buy firecrackers and set them off, and we often went to the Denver Country Club to watch their fabulous fireworks display. It was a joyful time filled with family and community spirit in Denver.

The Denver Country Club was a special place where my family celebrated holidays like the 4th of July. Located near Speer Boulevard and University, it hosted fireworks that were truly spectacular. Going there was a tradition and a highlight of our holiday celebrations in Denver.

The Weber Theatre was one of the movie theaters on South Broadway where I used to go as a teenager. To afford the movies, I would deliver flyers around the neighborhood and earn free tickets. It was a fun part of my youth, and I have fond memories of going to the Weber with my friends and family.

The Mayan Theatre was another favorite movie spot for me in Denver, located near First Avenue. Like the Weber, I helped distribute flyers to get free tickets to see movies there. It was part of the entertainment and social life I enjoyed during high school.

I once won a Shirley Temple doll at one of the movie theaters, which I treasured as a child. The theaters often gave away prizes, and that doll was very special to me, though I don’t remember what eventually happened to it. Shirley Temple was a beloved figure, and having that doll was a real joy.
